Page 1: Cover

Bearing 315
the newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
March-April 1988

What I Did On My Winter Vacation

by Anne York

In January of this year, Anne York and her husband, Harvey Friedman, traveled to the Asian Pacific Orienteering Carnival in Tasmania. Following are Anne's impressions of their trip. Paragraphs in italics are from the journal Anne kept during her trip.

I have an old souvenir pennant that my father sent me from Australia when I was two or three years old; it is blue felt and has a koala bear on it and I think I had wanted to visit Australia ever since I got that pennant. Wallabies, kangaroos, real koala bears, echidnas, and platypuses were some of my other reasons. Since Harvey hardly ever goes on a vacation-- especially an expensive vacation-- that lacks orienteering, it took more than my feminine wiles to convince him to go Down Under. I began my campaign for the trip in 1986 after Mike Schuh's club meeting presentation on the Hong Kong Asian Pacific Orienteering Carnival (APOC) (see, it is worthwhile to attend those meetings). Mike mentioned that the Aussies were hosting the APOC in early January 1988 on the island state of Tasmania. The carnival lasted over a week, with various clubs hosting different meets: a "pre-event," a "model event," the event, relays, a "post-event," and a mountain marathon.
My original goals were fulfilled in the first event of the Carnival-- the "pre-event" held a few miles south of Launceston in the northern part of Tasmania:

6 Jan. 1988 -- Tressick Hills, 10 km. S. of Launceston, Tasmania. I was running down the hill toward my next to last control, when I first heard the loud thumps and saw them jump; frankly, they scared me to death. I suppose it was my undisciplined lack of concentration but no worries mate (maite), they were the first wallabies I saw in the wild. I didn't worry about the lost minute or so that I spent watching them-- I had made a terrible 15 minute error about 3 controls before and I wasn't too concerned about losing another minute. At the moment, the animals were more interesting than the orienteering. They alternately jumped toward me or away from me depending on their distance to other competitors who were actually searching for their next to last control.

The "main event," the World Cup and Asian Pacific Orienteering Championships, was held at St. Helens on the east coast of Tasmania. The meet site was a beautiful open forest of gum and wattle trees (eucalyptus and acacia). The terrain featured complicated spur-gully systems with lots of rock features and open meadows. The hills were smooth and the gullies were not very steep; it was quite dry and there was hardly any underbrush, although some of the open meadows were difficult running because of large hummocks. It was a very interesting area; I had never orienteered on anything quite like it, although it had some similarities to other places. It was closest to the Mt. Laguna area near San Diego, but was more smooth and eroded-- sort of what Mt. Laguna might look like in 10 million years. The map was first class-- both in field work and cartography. I had a really good run-- perhaps losing only a minute with navigational mistakes. It was a fantastic feeling to hit so many controls. If I had only been faster ... However, not to complain too much, I did win a medal in D-45B:

Page 2

9 January 1988; St. Helens, Tasmania: It sounds schmaltzy but it was a great feeling when my name was called during today's award ceremony. "Anne York of the United States of America," the announcer said over the loudspeaker. I stepped up to the lowest position of the dais to receive my bronze medal with a Tasmanian devil holding an orienteering marker. Gave me an inkling of what someone feels when they win an Olympic medal. People were so nice-- "So happy someone besides an Aussie or a Scandie won something" or "You Yanks did quite well; half of you won a medal."

The competitors in the World Cup event were taken by bus to their start area very early in the morning. The event was organized so that lots of spectators could watch the World Cup finishers. The World Cup winners were all from Scandinavia Harald Tveite of Norway and Malin Westlund of Sweden were the winners. Ross Burnett (from British Columbia), the only North American running in the World Cup, finished in the middle of the pack.

The relays were held the following day. Most teams in the relays were from clubs, but some, like mine, was a mix. I had a terrible run but enjoyed myself anyway. It was a blisteringly hot day (40° C) and the heat took its toll on me. I had never run the first leg of a relay and the mass start was a zoo and I was gaga. It took me 45 minutes to find the first control. After that it went OK but neither I nor the rest of my team were pleased. The heat also affected the elite competition: the men's team from Switzerland upset the Scandinavian teams. The women's team from Sweden was able to uphold the honor of Scandinavia.
The post-event was held in southern Tasmania, near Hobart. Hobart is a beautiful city with a magnificent harbor, very reminiscent of Bergen, Norway. We were lucky to see many Tall Ships, which were visiting Hobart in honor of the Australian Bicentennial celebration. The orienteering took place on Seven-Mile Beach, an area of large and very intricate sand dunes. The map was at a 1:10000 scale with 2.5 meter contour interval. An added attraction of the post-event was the Australia-New Zealand Challenge. The mountain marathon took place in the mountains in the southwest of Tasmania. This is the only region in Australia which was glaciated during the last glaciation. I did not participate in the mountain marathon, but everyone who did said the area was fantastic. Ross Burnett (with his partner Roger Nicoll from Australia) won the elite event-- first prize was a round trip ticket to Nepal-- will we ever see Ross again?
One of the reasons that I participate in orienteering is for the opportunity to meet so many nice people. The APOC event was terrific from a people meeting perspective. The Aussies are really friendly and nice; the orienteers even more so. We received so many nice invitations from people we met during the events that we actually changed our planned itinerary and visited several of them in the last two weeks of our vacation. We saw some places we hadn't even considered visiting before, ran on several orienteering maps adjacent to our friends' homes, and socialized and traded maps from Adelaide to Ballarat to Sydney.

Page 3

1987 U.S. Rankings

It's that time of year again, when we find out how rank orienteers are. Several people from the Northwest were either ranked, or should have been ranked. Below are the names of the lucky few.

Name Class Club Ranking
Pat Albright F45-B Cascade 5
Harvey Friedman MOpen Or. Cascade 3
Carl Moore M50-A Nisqually 13
Debbie Newell F21-A Cascade 17
Katie Sprugel F21-B Cascade 3
Dave Tallent M35-B Samm. 4

Others who should have been ranked, but apparently are in orienteering limbo, were Arnold Kay (Cascade) in M21B, Anne York (Cascade) in F45A, Knut Olson (Cascade) in M60A, and Dave Enger (Sammamish) in M21A.

It's Easier To Get Ranked

Those of you who are interested in getting a U.S. Orienteering Federation ranking will find it easier to do this year. Last year, USOF members living in our fair state had to attend 4 days of U.S. "A" meets in order to be ranked. This year, you will be allowed to use 2 days of Canadian "A" meets to fulfill the minimum yearly requirement.
You must still attend (and complete the course in) 2 U.S. "A" meet days. But being able to use Canadian "A" meets will make it easier for Northwesterners to receive a USOF ranking. Our neighbors to the north in B.C. have scheduled no less than 10 days of "A" meets this year, while the nearest U.S. "A" meet is reportedly happening in the Bay Area (a mere 800 miles away) this fall. The best thing for us to do is hold our own "A" meet every year, and attend Canadian "A" meets.

Pot-O-Luck

The next pot-o-luck will be at 6:00 p.m. on March 12 (after the Lake Tradition meet) at the new home of Doug and Katie Sprugel. Bring your favorite dish to 10559 41st Pl. N.E., Seattle, and try all the other great food that is sure to appear. Katie, is becoming a legend in her own time when it comes to food, and is sure to make some delicious dish.

Lynndale Park Relay Update

The April 23 event at Lynndale Park in Lynnwood will feature a relay event instead of the usual advan ced courses, though we will still offer a novice course.
Teams will consist of two members, with each member completing two legs. Since the purpose of a relay is to build excitement, the courses will be fast and fairly easy.
Because a relay requires a mass start, it is very important that you be ready to start at 10:45 a.m. Please be there to register as close to 10:00 a.m. as possible. You can either team up with someone ahead of time, or show up and find a teammate at the meet.

General Meeting March 23

The next General Meeting will be held at the Green Lake Library, 7364 E. Green Lake Dr. N. at 6:45 p.m. on March 23. The meeting will feature a presentation on course setting by Mike Schuh. Slides of local orienteering meets will also be shown, so if you want to see yourself on the big screen, be sure to be there.

Western States Championships '89 Update

Sandy Kish Miller and Katie Sprugel have been named co-directors of next year's Western States Championships, to be held at Moran State Park on Orcas Island. Of course, they will need volunteers to make everything run smoothly, volunteers for course designing, vetting, timing, and other glamorous activities. It's never too early to volunteer, so beat the rush and volunteer now. Sandy can be reached at 782-1437 and Katie can be reached at 365-8742.

Sammamish Orienteering Club Cancels Hazelwood Meet

SAMM has been forced to cancel their March 6 meet at Hazelwood due to a lack of trees. The Hazelwood area is currently being turned into a housing development, and bulldozers are leveling trees left and right. So, you orienteering junkies will have to go cold turkey that weekend.

Falls To Gasworks Relay

The annual Falls to Gasworks Relay will be held on April 17. Teams of five runners (with at least 2 women) start at Mt. Si High School in Snoqualmie and run 75 kilometers to Gasworks Park in Seattle. Each team member runs two legs, each leg ranging in length from 3 to 7 miles. Last year was Cascade's first try at this relay, and all the runners seemed to enjoy it. If you are interested in running this year, call Debbie Newell by March 31 at 488-7471.

Orienteering Instructor Needed

The Redmond Parks and Recreation Department is looking for someone to lead basic skills classes in orienteering for youth and adults. The classes are scheduled for summer at Farrel-McWhirter Park in Redmond. This is a paying position, and the rate is negotiable. If you are interested, call Sandra Bettencourt by March 11 at 882-6401.

Page 4

Orienteering Hotline

Have you ever wanted to know when the next orienteering meet was, but couldn't find your schedule? Well, now you can call the Cascade Orienteering Club's Orienteering Hotline to get the latest information about upcoming meets.
With the help of a grant from the U.S. Orienteering Federation, we have purchased an answering machine and now have our own telephone number (it's even in the new phone book!). The line is installed in a club member's home, and the machine's message will be updated regularly, and will also allow you to leave messages. The new phone number is 783-3866, and is scheduled to be in operation on March 1. But you know how the phone company is, so if you try this number and it doesn't work, call Mike Schuh (783-3960) and ask him about it.

Orienteering Returns to Oregon

After an absence of several years, Oregon Orienteering has been reincarnated, and will hold a two-day meet at Spring Creek, near Bend, on April 30 and May 1. The meet will be run on a black and white 1:15,000 scale map, and will be organized by Jeff Lewis. If you want more information, give Jeff a call at (503) 254-9578.

Presidential Ramblings

by Mike Schuh

Traditionally, the end of one year and the beginning of the next is a time to reflect on our accomplishments and contemplate the future. Last year was my first year as President of COC; this year will be my second. In retrospect, the past year had many good things happen, though not as many as had been hoped for.
Some of my goals for 1987 were: to have at least $1,000 in our treasury at year's end, to boost average meet attendance to at least 100, and to have at least one meet be run completely by new meet staff. Our meet attendance has topped 100 only a few times in our existence (in 1987, only at the Washington State Championships), but the other two goals were met.
My goals for 1988? Well, I'd still like to see our meet attendance increase, and the other two goals from 1987 -- healthy finances and a broadening base of volunteers -- are still worth striving for. My goals -- desires, dreams, visions, aspirations, whatever -- are only mine. What are yours? If we -- all of us -- can agree on a goal, then we can move towards it with a unanimity that is unstoppable. The future is what we make it, and is ours for the shaping.
A bit more down to earth, I'd like to recognize some of the club members who have contributed to the club's success during 1987. I hope I have not overlooked anyone. Debbie Newell has edited and produced our newsletter all year, and contributed to the 10th Anniversary booklet. Doug Sprugel was our Treasurer and, with his wife Katie, directed the Washington State Championships at Trollhaugen. They also helped organize our trip to Moran State Park. Harvey Friedman has maintained our membership records and has taken the lead in our venture to host a major event in 1990 (originally intended to be a part of the Goodwill Games, now hoping to be a World Cup event). Arnold Kay has kept our equipment in good shape and has set courses and directed meets. John Huth did the fieldchecking for the Fort Casey map, and served as Mapping Coordinator until moving to Nevada. Len Englund was our Vice President until October, and was responsible for organizing our meets. He has since left to join the Seattle Goodwill Games organization, where I'm sure his contributions will be as appreciated as they were with us. Robin Harvard has been our Secretary and has also made the arrangements for meeting rooms for club meetings. Judy Cohen organized our Pot-O-Luck social events and helped with our 10th Anniversary banquet. Betty Berreth wore out several telephones and both ears calling club members to solicit volunteers. She did such a good job that to list all of the members who did help at our meets last year would double the length of this article -- THANKS, Betty. Pat Albright helped sew our meet staff vests, helped with the 10th Anniversary booklet, and designed the Washington State O' Association logo. Rick Hood, who is now in Belgium, contributed greatly to the 10th Anniversary celebration -- it simply would not have happened without him.

Many members directed meets this year, some for the first time. Among them: Don Atkinson, Eileen Breseman, Ardis Dull, Len Englund, Harvey Friedman, John Huth, Arnold Kay, Bruce McAlister, Debbie Newell, Doug Sprugel, and Katie Sprugel.
Many course setters were also first-timers: Pat Albright, Don Atkinson, Jack Raidy, and Tony Saulewicz.
Of special note are the courses designed by Ron Pontius for the State Championships at Trollhaugen. Knowing that he would be leaving the state before the event, he offered to design the courses, thereby allowing nearly everyone from the state a chance to run. Thank you, Ron!
At the next meet you attend, thank the meet staff. They made the event possible. Then please consider helping out at a later event so that they can participate in it. Our club would not exist without volunteers. With volunteers, we can do many wonderful things, and 1988 will bring us a lot of fun.

The Pontius Arrowhead

by Bob Reddick

Decades from now, as our children's children ponder over the arcane history and lore of their ancestors' rudimentary attempts at orienteering, questions may arise over the origin of the lonely little mark stuck by itself down at the south end of O' maps. Well, for those future researchers who locate this hallowed publication, here is the story. It seems that one Ron Pontius, a famous and very fast M21 elite type, member of the notorious Blue Star Komplex, and while affiliated with the Nisqually O' Club of Olympia-Tacoma, once ran a championship relay at the equally infamous Forest of Nisene Marks in California. At an early control, his compass and folded map somehow slipped in his hand, and when he reset them for his next leg, a ghastly 180-degree error developed! By the time he caught it, all hope for his relay team had been wiped out!
All for the lack of a visible arrowhead? Yes, it's true! If this happened to an elite orienteer, imagine what the rest of the orienteers were doing after folding up those big maps to manageable size to grasp neatly with a thumb compass on top. The magnetic north lines are carefully spaced across the map, and names are north-side up, but those arrowheads often disappear from sight as maps are turned and folded. Thus, north becomes south, but only at the most inopportune times. Murphy never heard of this one!
Some astute orienteers had for some time been placing supplemental arrowheads here and there on their maps during events, but it occurred to the author, hearing Ron's sad story while making my first map, that a little arrowhead or two belonged down at the south end of those lines, at some likely spot(s) which would remain visible even if the entire north end of the map got turned under.
Well, the rest is history. Sure enough, other Northwest U.S. mapmakers adopted this simple convention, and little by little the thing spread, and the Pontius arrowhead name stuck. Eventually, it will win IOF approval (after several decades). No one ever heard of Ron again, but his namesake, the Pontius Arrowhead, has made him immortal.

block_index.text: Bearing 315 the newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club March-April 1988
What I Did On My Winter Vacation

In January of this year, Anne York and her husband, Harvey Friedman, traveled to the Asian Pacific Orienteering Carnival in Tasmania. Following are Anne's impressions of their trip. Paragraphs in italics are from the journal Anne kept during her trip.

I have an old souvenir pennant that my father sent me from Australia when I was two or three years old; it is blue felt and has a koala bear on it and I think I had wanted to visit Australia ever since I got that pennant. Wallabies, kangaroos, real koala bears, echidnas, and platypuses were some of my other reasons. Since Harvey hardly ever goes on a vacation-- especially an expensive vacation-- that lacks orienteering, it took more than my feminine wiles to convince him to go Down Under. I began my campaign for the trip in 1986 after Mike Schuh's club meeting presentation on the Hong Kong Asian Pacific Orienteering Carnival (APOC) (see, it is worthwhile to attend those meetings). Mike mentioned that the Aussies were hosting the APOC in early January 1988 on the island state of Tasmania. The carnival lasted over a week, with various clubs hosting different meets: a "pre-event," a "model event," the event, relays, a "post-event," and a mountain marathon. My original goals were fulfilled in the first event of the Carnival-- the "pre-event" held a few miles south of Launceston in the northern part of Tasmania:

6 Jan. 1988 -- Tressick Hills, 10 km. S. of Launceston, Tasmania. I was running down the hill toward my next to last control, when I first heard the loud thumps and saw them jump; frankly, they scared me to death. I suppose it was my undisciplined lack of concentration but no worries mate (maite), they were the first wallabies I saw in the wild. I didn't worry about the lost minute or so that I spent watching them-- I had made a terrible 15 minute error about 3 controls before and I wasn't too concerned about losing another minute. At the moment, the animals were more interesting than the orienteering. They alternately jumped toward me or away from me depending on their distance to other competitors who were actually searching for their next to last control.

The "main event," the World Cup and Asian Pacific Orienteering Championships, was held at St. Helens on the east coast of Tasmania. The meet site was a beautiful open forest of gum and wattle trees (eucalyptus and acacia). The terrain featured complicated spur-gully systems with lots of rock features and open meadows. The hills were smooth and the gullies were not very steep; it was quite dry and there was hardly any underbrush, although some of the open meadows were difficult running because of large hummocks. It was a very interesting area; I had never orienteered on anything quite like it, although it had some similarities to other places. It was closest to the Mt. Laguna area near San Diego, but was more smooth and eroded-- sort of what Mt. Laguna might look like in 10 million years. The map was first class-- both in field work and cartography. I had a really good run-- perhaps losing only a minute with navigational mistakes. It was a fantastic feeling to hit so many controls. If I had only been faster ... However, not to complain too much, I did win a medal in D-45B:

9 January 1988; St. Helens, Tasmania: It sounds schmaltzy but it was a great feeling when my name was called during today's award ceremony. "Anne York of the United States of America," the announcer said over the loudspeaker. I stepped up to the lowest position of the dais to receive my bronze medal with a Tasmanian devil holding an orienteering marker. Gave me an inkling of what someone feels when they win an Olympic medal. People were so nice-- "So happy someone besides an Aussie or a Scandie won something" or "You Yanks did quite well; half of you won a medal."

The competitors in the World Cup event were taken by bus to their start area very early in the morning. The event was organized so that lots of spectators could watch the World Cup finishers. The World Cup winners were all from Scandinavia Harald Tveite of Norway and Malin Westlund of Sweden were the winners. Ross Burnett (from British Columbia), the only North American running in the World Cup, finished in the middle of the pack.

The relays were held the following day. Most teams in the relays were from clubs, but some, like mine, was a mix. I had a terrible run but enjoyed myself anyway. It was a blisteringly hot day (40° C) and the heat took its toll on me. I had never run the first leg of a relay and the mass start was a zoo and I was gaga. It took me 45 minutes to find the first control. After that it went OK but neither I nor the rest of my team were pleased. The heat also affected the elite competition: the men's team from Switzerland upset the Scandinavian teams. The women's team from Sweden was able to uphold the honor of Scandinavia. The post-event was held in southern Tasmania, near Hobart. Hobart is a beautiful city with a magnificent harbor, very reminiscent of Bergen, Norway. We were lucky to see many Tall Ships, which were visiting Hobart in honor of the Australian Bicentennial celebration. The orienteering took place on Seven-Mile Beach, an area of large and very intricate sand dunes. The map was at a 1:10000 scale with 2.5 meter contour interval. An added attraction of the post-event was the Australia-New Zealand Challenge. The mountain marathon took place in the mountains in the southwest of Tasmania. This is the only region in Australia which was glaciated during the last glaciation. I did not participate in the mountain marathon, but everyone who did said the area was fantastic. Ross Burnett (with his partner Roger Nicoll from Australia) won the elite event-- first prize was a round trip ticket to Nepal-- will we ever see Ross again? One of the reasons that I participate in orienteering is for the opportunity to meet so many nice people. The APOC event was terrific from a people meeting perspective. The Aussies are really friendly and nice; the orienteers even more so. We received so many nice invitations from people we met during the events that we actually changed our planned itinerary and visited several of them in the last two weeks of our vacation. We saw some places we hadn't even considered visiting before, ran on several orienteering maps adjacent to our friends' homes, and socialized and traded maps from Adelaide to Ballarat to Sydney.
It's that time of year again, when we find out how rank orienteers are. Several people from the Northwest were either ranked, or should have been ranked. Below are the names of the lucky few.

Others who should have been ranked, but apparently are in orienteering limbo, were Arnold Kay (Cascade) in M21B, Anne York (Cascade) in F45A, Knut Olson (Cascade) in M60A, and Dave Enger (Sammamish) in M21A.

Those of you who are interested in getting a U.S. Orienteering Federation ranking will find it easier to do this year. Last year, USOF members living in our fair state had to attend 4 days of U.S. "A" meets in order to be ranked. This year, you will be allowed to use 2 days of Canadian "A" meets to fulfill the minimum yearly requirement. You must still attend (and complete the course in) 2 U.S. "A" meet days. But being able to use Canadian "A" meets will make it easier for Northwesterners to receive a USOF ranking. Our neighbors to the north in B.C. have scheduled no less than 10 days of "A" meets this year, while the nearest U.S. "A" meet is reportedly happening in the Bay Area (a mere 800 miles away) this fall. The best thing for us to do is hold our own "A" meet every year, and attend Canadian "A" meets.

The next pot-o-luck will be at 6:00 p.m. on March 12 (after the Lake Tradition meet) at the new home of Doug and Katie Sprugel. Bring your favorite dish to 10559 41st Pl. N.E., Seattle, and try all the other great food that is sure to appear. Katie, is becoming a legend in her own time when it comes to food, and is sure to make some delicious dish.

The April 23 event at Lynndale Park in Lynnwood will feature a relay event instead of the usual advan ced courses, though we will still offer a novice course. Teams will consist of two members, with each member completing two legs. Since the purpose of a relay is to build excitement, the courses will be fast and fairly easy. Because a relay requires a mass start, it is very important that you be ready to start at 10:45 a.m. Please be there to register as close to 10:00 a.m. as possible. You can either team up with someone ahead of time, or show up and find a teammate at the meet.

The next General Meeting will be held at the Green Lake Library, 7364 E. Green Lake Dr. N. at 6:45 p.m. on March 23. The meeting will feature a presentation on course setting by Mike Schuh. Slides of local orienteering meets will also be shown, so if you want to see yourself on the big screen, be sure to be there.

Sandy Kish Miller and Katie Sprugel have been named co-directors of next year's Western States Championships, to be held at Moran State Park on Orcas Island. Of course, they will need volunteers to make everything run smoothly, volunteers for course designing, vetting, timing, and other glamorous activities. It's never too early to volunteer, so beat the rush and volunteer now. Sandy can be reached at 782-1437 and Katie can be reached at 365-8742.
Sammamish Orienteering Club Cancels Hazelwood Meet

SAMM has been forced to cancel their March 6 meet at Hazelwood due to a lack of trees. The Hazelwood area is currently being turned into a housing development, and bulldozers are leveling trees left and right. So, you orienteering junkies will have to go cold turkey that weekend.

The annual Falls to Gasworks Relay will be held on April 17. Teams of five runners (with at least 2 women) start at Mt. Si High School in Snoqualmie and run 75 kilometers to Gasworks Park in Seattle. Each team member runs two legs, each leg ranging in length from 3 to 7 miles. Last year was Cascade's first try at this relay, and all the runners seemed to enjoy it. If you are interested in running this year, call Debbie Newell by March 31 at 488-7471.

The Redmond Parks and Recreation Department is looking for someone to lead basic skills classes in orienteering for youth and adults. The classes are scheduled for summer at Farrel-McWhirter Park in Redmond. This is a paying position, and the rate is negotiable. If you are interested, call Sandra Bettencourt by March 11 at 882-6401.

Have you ever wanted to know when the next orienteering meet was, but couldn't find your schedule? Well, now you can call the Cascade Orienteering Club's Orienteering Hotline to get the latest information about upcoming meets. With the help of a grant from the U.S. Orienteering Federation, we have purchased an answering machine and now have our own telephone number (it's even in the new phone book!). The line is installed in a club member's home, and the machine's message will be updated regularly, and will also allow you to leave messages. The new phone number is 783-3866, and is scheduled to be in operation on March 1. But you know how the phone company is, so if you try this number and it doesn't work, call Mike Schuh (783-3960) and ask him about it.

After an absence of several years, Oregon Orienteering has been reincarnated, and will hold a two-day meet at Spring Creek, near Bend, on April 30 and May 1. The meet will be run on a black and white 1:15,000 scale map, and will be organized by Jeff Lewis. If you want more information, give Jeff a call at (503) 254-9578.

Traditionally, the end of one year and the beginning of the next is a time to reflect on our accomplishments and contemplate the future. Last year was my first year as President of COC; this year will be my second. In retrospect, the past year had many good things happen, though not as many as had been hoped for. Some of my goals for 1987 were: to have at least $1,000 in our treasury at year's end, to boost average meet attendance to at least 100, and to have at least one meet be run completely by new meet staff. Our meet attendance has topped 100 only a few times in our existence (in 1987, only at the Washington State Championships), but the other two goals were met. My goals for 1988? Well, I'd still like to see our meet attendance increase, and the other two goals from 1987 -- healthy finances and a broadening base of volunteers -- are still worth striving for. My goals -- desires, dreams, visions, aspirations, whatever -- are only mine. What are yours? If we -- all of us -- can agree on a goal, then we can move towards it with a unanimity that is unstoppable. The future is what we make it, and is ours for the shaping. A bit more down to earth, I'd like to recognize some of the club members who have contributed to the club's success during 1987. I hope I have not overlooked anyone. Debbie Newell has edited and produced our newsletter all year, and contributed to the 10th Anniversary booklet. Doug Sprugel was our Treasurer and, with his wife Katie, directed the Washington State Championships at Trollhaugen. They also helped organize our trip to Moran State Park. Harvey Friedman has maintained our membership records and has taken the lead in our venture to host a major event in 1990 (originally intended to be a part of the Goodwill Games, now hoping to be a World Cup event). Arnold Kay has kept our equipment in good shape and has set courses and directed meets. John Huth did the fieldchecking for the Fort Casey map, and served as Mapping Coordinator until moving to Nevada. Len Englund was our Vice President until October, and was responsible for organizing our meets. He has since left to join the Seattle Goodwill Games organization, where I'm sure his contributions will be as appreciated as they were with us. Robin Harvard has been our Secretary and has also made the arrangements for meeting rooms for club meetings. Judy Cohen organized our Pot-O-Luck social events and helped with our 10th Anniversary banquet. Betty Berreth wore out several telephones and both ears calling club members to solicit volunteers. She did such a good job that to list all of the members who did help at our meets last year would double the length of this article -- THANKS, Betty. Pat Albright helped sew our meet staff vests, helped with the 10th Anniversary booklet, and designed the Washington State O' Association logo. Rick Hood, who is now in Belgium, contributed greatly to the 10th Anniversary celebration -- it simply would not have happened without him.

Many members directed meets this year, some for the first time. Among them: Don Atkinson, Eileen Breseman, Ardis Dull, Len Englund, Harvey Friedman, John Huth, Arnold Kay, Bruce McAlister, Debbie Newell, Doug Sprugel, and Katie Sprugel. Many course setters were also first-timers: Pat Albright, Don Atkinson, Jack Raidy, and Tony Saulewicz. Of special note are the courses designed by Ron Pontius for the State Championships at Trollhaugen. Knowing that he would be leaving the state before the event, he offered to design the courses, thereby allowing nearly everyone from the state a chance to run. Thank you, Ron! At the next meet you attend, thank the meet staff. They made the event possible. Then please consider helping out at a later event so that they can participate in it. Our club would not exist without volunteers. With volunteers, we can do many wonderful things, and 1988 will bring us a lot of fun.

Island Crest Park - December 5, 1988 Sammamish Orienteering Club Meet Director: Dave Enger Course Setter: Bob Rein Registration: Dave Cook Start: John Lilly Finish: Dave Lilly Refreshments: Valerie Enger Weather: steady rain, cool

Course 3 (score, 22 controls) Time limit of 25 minutes, 3 point penalty for each minute overtime, 28 pts. maximum.

Decades from now, as our children's children ponder over the arcane history and lore of their ancestors' rudimentary attempts at orienteering, questions may arise over the origin of the lonely little mark stuck by itself down at the south end of O' maps. Well, for those future researchers who locate this hallowed publication, here is the story. It seems that one Ron Pontius, a famous and very fast M21 elite type, member of the notorious Blue Star Komplex, and while affiliated with the Nisqually O' Club of Olympia-Tacoma, once ran a championship relay at the equally infamous Forest of Nisene Marks in California. At an early control, his compass and folded map somehow slipped in his hand, and when he reset them for his next leg, a ghastly 180-degree error developed! By the time he caught it, all hope for his relay team had been wiped out! All for the lack of a visible arrowhead? Yes, it's true! If this happened to an elite orienteer, imagine what the rest of the orienteers were doing after folding up those big maps to manageable size to grasp neatly with a thumb compass on top. The magnetic north lines are carefully spaced across the map, and names are north-side up, but those arrowheads often disappear from sight as maps are turned and folded. Thus, north becomes south, but only at the most inopportune times. Murphy never heard of this one! Some astute orienteers had for some time been placing supplemental arrowheads here and there on their maps during events, but it occurred to the author, hearing Ron's sad story while making my first map, that a little arrowhead or two belonged down at the south end of those lines, at some likely spot(s) which would remain visible even if the entire north end of the map got turned under. Well, the rest is history. Sure enough, other Northwest U.S. mapmakers adopted this simple convention, and little by little the thing spread, and the Pontius arrowhead name stuck. Eventually, it will win IOF approval (after several decades). No one ever heard of Ron again, but his namesake, the Pontius Arrowhead, has made him immortal.
